key_insight: A unified planar 3D ILSA solver, combined with matrix splitting and Anderson acceleration, substantially reduces hydraulic-fracture simulation time while keeping fracture-aperture error low.
engineering_relevance: These speedups make planar 3D hydraulic-fracture models more practical for design optimization and mini-frac interpretation, where many simulations are needed.
method: Consolidate the elastohydrodynamic and front-tracking loops into one iterative unified planar 3D ILSA scheme.
Use matrix splitting to move the dense elasticity contribution to the right-hand side, leaving a sparse system to solve.
Apply Anderson acceleration, optionally with a predictor-corrector step, to improve convergence as fracture geometry evolves.
evidence_signal: Across five benchmark cases, the unified scheme averaged 2.5× speedup, the full combination averaged 4×, and the sandglass case reached 11× speedup with fracture-aperture discrepancy below 5%.
watch_item: The acceleration benefits may depend strongly on fracture geometry and solver coupling, so robustness should be checked case by case before deployment.
paper_title: Acceleration methods for the planar 3D ILSA hydraulic fracturing model
